Men's 100m Backstroke' event, accompanied by a more motivational, electronic pop track. Swimmers prepare at the brightly lit Rio 2016 Pool, adjusting goggles and caps, including an American swimmer, Ryan Murphy, in lane 4, and a Romanian swimmer in a white 'ROU' cap. A male commentator notes that Ryan Murphy in lane 4 had been just 1/100th of a second faster than David Plummer in the semi-finals, and that Murphy and Plummer were swimming side by side. The race begins with a dive, met by loud crowd cheering and splashing sounds. Underwater and overhead shots capture the powerful backstroke movements. The commentator highlights that Plummer, swimming in the middle lanes with yellow lane ropes, made the stronger start. As the swimmers approach the 50m mark, on-screen graphics display split times, including 'WR SPLIT 25.35 -0.17' and '50M', along with rankings for M. Larkin (AUS), Xu J. (CHN), and D. Plummer (USA). The commentator further explains that Murphy wasn't even in a medal position at the halfway turn, with Mitchell Larkin of Australia leading at that stage, all while the intense crowd cheering and splashing continue, adding to the competitive atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The Men's 100m Backstroke final at the Rio 2016 Olympics opens with an overhead view of the pool, capturing swimmers mid-race amidst the roar of the crowd and splashing water. Ryan Murphy (USA, lane 4), initially behind, executes an 'incredible turn' to surface in the lead, a tactic the commentator notes he used successfully in the semi-finals. While Xu J. (CHN, lane 2) and M. Larkin (AUS, lane 3) led at the 50m split, Murphy makes a dramatic surge, swimming 'close to world record pace' in the second half, leaving his rivals unable to fight back. The Omega Timer confirms his victory with a final time of '51.97' seconds, which the commentator proudly declares is a 'new Olympic record,' just 3/100ths of a second outside the world record. Murphy crosses the finish line first, followed by Xu J. and D. Plummer (USA, lane 5). The scene shifts to Murphy celebrating in the pool, then to the victory ceremony where he receives his gold medal. The commentator announces silver for 'Zu Jiayu of China' and bronze for 'David Plummer of the United States.' Uplifting, emotional background music with male vocals begins as Murphy stands on the podium alongside Xu J. and D. Plummer. The video concludes with a text overlay highlighting Murphy’s comeback and Plummer’s achievement at 30 in his first Olympics, before transitioning to the next event, the Women's 400m IM, with a blurred pool view and title card.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Hungarian swimmer Katinka Hosszú, identified by the commentator as "the favorite" in lane 4, wearing a white cap with 'HUN' and a red jacket, preparing at the starting block for the Women’s 400m Individual Medley at the Rio 2016 Olympics. The commentator notes this is her "fourth Olympic Games" and she had "never won a medal" before. She dives into the pool with powerful form, transitioning smoothly into freestyle, breaststroke, backstroke, and butterfly. The camera follows her from above as she dominates the race, maintaining a significant lead over competitors like Maya Dirado (USA) and Emily Overholt (CAN), who trail by over 3 seconds at the 200m mark. The commentator highlights her strong start, pulling away during the 100m backstroke. The Omega timer shows her split times, and as she approaches the finish, the on-screen display updates to show her breaking the World Record with a time of 4:26.36. The commentator confirms her time of "4 minutes 26.36 seconds was more than two seconds faster than the previous world record," as the crowd's excitement builds into roaring cheers. After touching the wall, she emerges from the pool, visibly emotional, and is later shown on the podium in a red 'HUNGARY' jacket, holding her gold medal with pride. The victory ceremony features her flanked by silver medalist Maya Dirado (USA) and bronze medalist Mireia Belmonte García (ESP), both explicitly named by the commentator, all smiling under the Olympic rings. Text overlays highlight her achievement: winning gold after three Olympic Games and being at least 4 seconds ahead of the field. The segment transitions to the Women’s 800m Freestyle, with a title card appearing over the pool, signaling the next event, accompanied by uplifting, emotional background music featuring a female vocalist.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Women's 800m Freestyle final at the Rio 2016 Olympics, showcasing the vibrant blue Rio 2016 Pool with colorful lane dividers and visible Rio 2016 branding. The camera alternates between underwater, side-angle, and overhead views, capturing Ledecky (USA, lane 4) in her black Speedo cap and USA swimsuit, leading decisively. At the 50m mark, Ledecky's split is 28.03, just under the world record split of 28.35. The female commentator notes Ledecky "sprinting out in the first 100 meters," mentioning her 57.98 split in the 800m doesn't "cost her a lot" compared to her 52-second relay split. J. Carlin (GBR, lane 3), wearing a black cap with 'GBR', follows in second, with the male commentator identifying her as "Carlin of Great Britain" and describing her as "the woman in the red cap" (despite her visible black cap). By 100m, Ledecky maintains her lead with a 57.98 split, while Carlin is +1.91 behind and M. The Omega Timer and on-screen graphics consistently display Ledecky's dominance. As the male commentator states, Ledecky "cruises out over 150 to a two-body length lead," with Carlin now +2.19 behind and Belmonte García +2.79 behind, showcasing her powerful strokes and breath control. The crowd's presence adds to the electric atmosphere as the female commentator praises Ledecky's technique, even "world records aside."

The commentators explicitly state, 'Ladecky leads in lane 4,' and describe her as 'driving away.' As the race progresses towards the 250m mark, where the WR SPLIT is 2:31.03 and her current time is 2:29.0, Ledecky extends her lead, prompting commentators to shift focus to 'who's likely to finish second' as 'the battle continues' behind her. Underwater shots reveal the swimmers' streamlined movements and powerful kicks, while Rio 2016 branding is visible on the pool walls and starting blocks, underscoring the intense Olympic atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The video captures the final stretch of the Women’s 800m Freestyle at the Rio 2016 Olympics, focusing on K. Ledecky (USA) in lane 4 as she powerfully dominates the race, with commentators noting she's "racing against herself" and anticipating a new world record. The overhead view shows the Olympic-sized pool with vibrant lane dividers, while the Omega timer tracks her progress, displaying splits like 7:37.84 at 750m, and a commentator highlights a "30.76" lap. Ledecky, wearing a black Speedo cap with 'LEDECKY' and a USA-branded swimsuit, maintains a commanding lead, with commentators emphasizing her distance from competitors, stating they "won't even be able to see the feet of Katie Ledecky." As she approaches the finish, the crowd's roar builds, with commentators confirming the "crowd urges her on." She crosses the finish line at 8:04.79, breaking the World Record of 8:06.68, which is immediately confirmed by on-screen graphics displaying 'NEW WR' and her name alongside the American flag, while commentators excitedly declare "Ledecky, gold medal! Ledecky, world record! Katie Ledecky of the USA reigns supreme yet again!" and confirm the time as "a new world record, a new Olympic record." A brief underwater shot captures Ledecky’s streamlined form and strong kick. Behind her, J. Carlin (GBR) in lane 3 and B. Kapas (HUN) in lane 5 trail, with commentators confirming Carlin wins the silver medal for Great Britain and Kapas the bronze for Hungary, also mentioning other trailing swimmers. The atmosphere is electric, with bright lighting illuminating the pool and the celebratory reactions of spectators, who are seen waving flags and recording the moment, their cheers erupting into a crescendo as Ledecky touches the wall.

The commentator continues, highlighting her historical achievement: "That makes it the 200, the 400, and the 800. Not since Debbie Meyer in 1968 has that happened. And so, again, we're seeing history in the pool." A female voice briefly adds to the commentary. The scene transitions to the Victory Ceremony, where K. Ledecky stands on the top podium step, flanked by Jazz Carlin (GBR, silver) and Boglarka Kapas (HUN, bronze), all holding their medals and smiling, with the commentator noting "tears flowing for Katie Ledecky." A text overlay emphasizes her dominance, breaking her own world record by almost 2 seconds and beating the rest of the field by at least 11 seconds. Upbeat pop/rock music with vocals then plays, featuring lyrics like "Until I broke the rules, my life destroyed," as the segment concludes with a transition to the next event, the Men’s 100m Breaststroke, shown with a title card over the pool and a brief shot of swimmers on the starting blocks, including one in a blue cap with 'AUS' and another in a black cap with 'USA', poised for the race, before the music fades and the crowd noise returns briefly.

segments.integrated_caption: The high-intensity Men's 100m Breaststroke final at the Rio 2016 Olympics erupts with a loud crowd cheer and splash as swimmers dive into the vibrant Rio 2016 Pool. An underwater view captures their synchronized power, transitioning to an overhead shot as they surface. Commentator 1 immediately exclaims, "Peaty, brilliant off the blocks," though noting that C. VAN DER BURGH, who "gets away very well," and K. CORDES in lane 2 are also strong contenders. The Omega timer begins tracking the race, displaying split times and rankings, while Commentator 1 identifies "Cody Miller in five" among the leading pack. As A. PEATY, in lane 4, executes his powerful breaststroke, Commentator 2 enthusiastically states, "once he hits the water, Adam Peaty just flies," praising his "so fast" stroke rate and observing he's "fingertipping the world record line." At the 50-meter mark, the Omega timer shows Peaty's split at 26.61 seconds, with Commentator 2 confirming he's "below it by 8100s of a second." Despite C. VAN DER BURGH "trying to go with him," Peaty's dominance is undeniable. Commentator 1 declares Peaty, who has "already set a world record here," is "stunning," with "three-quarters of a body length clear" and "25 meters to go," asserting he's "got this race won already" and is "in a class of his own." The on-screen Text Overlay confirms a 'NEW WR' as PEATY crosses the finish line in a record-breaking 57.13 seconds, securing "a gold medal to the Great Britain team," a feat not achieved by Britain in this event since 1988. The electric atmosphere is palpable, with the Crowd Reaction visible in the stands, waving flags and recording the historic moment, while the commentators' excited voices underscore the thrilling victory.

A male commentator notes, "There's quite a few lining up now. This is not clear cut at all," as the Omega timer tracks the race, displaying on-screen graphics for S. MANUEL in lane 3, P. OLEKSIK in lane 5, and S. SJOSTROM in lane 6. As the race concludes, the scoreboard shows P. OLEKSIK winning with a time of 52.70, setting a new Olympic Record, while S. MANUEL finishes in second. However, commentators express confusion, with a female voice exclaiming, "It's a dead heat! Oh my goodness!" and elaborating, "We have dual Olympic champions... and it's happened again in 2016," while a male commentator speculates they might have "tossed a coin for who would get the first medal." The female commentator also confirms S. SJOSTROM "got into third place." The camera then cuts to close-ups of the swimmers at the wall: S. MANUEL, wearing a black cap with 'MANUEL' and an American flag, raises her arm in celebration, while P. OLEKSIK, in a white cap with 'OLEKSIK' and a Canadian maple leaf, looks exhausted but triumphant. The two athletes share a moment of sportsmanship, touching hands in the water. The scene transitions to the victory ceremony, set to an upbeat, inspirational pop song with male vocals singing "Can I be your superhero?" A French announcer is briefly heard. S. MANUEL (gold), P. OLEKSIK (silver), and S. SJOSTROM (bronze) stand on the podium, smiling and holding their medals. Text overlays highlight Simone Manuel as the first African American to win an individual Olympic gold in swimming, and Penny Oleksiak, at 16, becoming the most successful Canadian athlete in a single summer Olympics by winning her fourth medal. The segment ends with a title card for the Men’s 200m Butterfly, followed by a shot of Chad Guy Bertrand LE CLOS (RSA) in a green cap and Michael Phelps (USA) in a black cap with 'PHELPS' and an American flag, both preparing for their event, as a male commentator identifies them: "Le Clo of South Africa was swimming in lane six. Michael Phelps of the United States in lane five," with crowd noise audible in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of swimmers, including Michael Phelps in lane 5 with a black cap featuring 'PHELPS' and the American flag, poised on starting blocks at the vibrant Rio 2016 Olympic pool for the Men’s 200m Butterfly final. A distinct starting buzzer sounds, and the swimmers dive in unison, creating a splash as they begin their underwater dolphin kicks. The commentator immediately sets the scene, noting that Chad Guy Bertrand Le Clos had won this event at London 2012, narrowly beating Phelps. As the camera shifts to an overhead view, tracking the swimmers' powerful butterfly strokes, the commentator observes Phelps looking powerful in the first 50 meters, even though he initially touched just behind L. Cseh. On-screen graphics and the Omega timer display split times, showing Phelps quickly taking control and leading at the 50m mark. The intense, rhythmic movements of the swimmers are captured through alternating overhead and underwater shots, with the commentator confirming Phelps's lead as they approach the halfway point. The crowd's excitement builds with a palpable roar as the commentator updates that Le Clos turned in third place, more than half a second behind. By 150m, Phelps maintains his decisive lead, with the commentator stating he was still leading as they turned for the final time. In the final stretch, Phelps pulls ahead, and the commentator notes Le Clos, visible in his green cap, was keeping his eye on Phelps but ultimately "didn't have the strength to close the gap." Phelps touches the wall first with a time of 1:53.36, breaking the Olympic Record and setting a new World Record, confirmed by on-screen graphics. The commentator exclaims that Phelps held everybody off and was "number one in the 200 meters butterfly once again." Phelps emerges from the water, raising both arms in triumph with a joyful expression, celebrating his gold medal victory amidst the explosive roar of the crowd, as the commentator begins to describe his reclamation of the title.

The camera swiftly transitions to an overhead shot, showing the swimmers in their respective lanes, marked by vibrant dividers. Commentators identify Pereira, "third from your right, towards the top of the screen," as he edges slightly in front, swimming "alongside Phelps" and Lochte, confirming "they're all in a line." The Omega Timer displays the race progress, with Michael Phelps in lane 4 (black cap, 'PHELPS', American flag), T. Pereira in lane 3 (green cap, 'BRA'), and R. Lochte in lane 5 (black cap, 'LOCHTE'). Despite commentators mistakenly referring to "backstroke" and "breaststroke" at various points, the swimmers are clearly executing powerful butterfly strokes, creating splashes and bubbles. The crowd's excitement is palpable, with commentators stating, "The crowd will go crazy! They do!" The camera alternates between overhead and underwater views, capturing the intense competition and synchronized movements. Commentators also praise Phelps' "underwaters" and warn, "Don't write off Ryan Lochte," mentioning he's a "past champion." By the 100m mark, the timer reads 53.44, with Lochte now leading slightly, followed closely by Pereira and Phelps, all within 0.01 seconds of each other, swimming at "world record pace." The Rio 2016 branding is visible on the pool walls as the race remains tightly contested, setting the stage for a dramatic finish.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Michael Phelps, wearing a black swim cap, emerging visibly exhausted from the Rio 2016 Pool after his Men's 200m Individual Medley win, as the crowd roars and a commentator exclaims, "Extraordinary, yet again." The Omega Timer displays his winning time of 1:54.66, with an on-screen graphic confirming his victory, and the commentator emphasizing the significant lead: "1.95 seconds was the margin in victory." The commentator continues to laud his achievement, stating, "Four times he wins the 200 meters individual medley, the first swimmer... No one has achieved... No one's got close to achieving what he has," adding, "Remember he thought three was enough." The scene transitions to the victory ceremony, where Phelps, now in a navy USA jacket, receives his gold medal amidst continued crowd cheers and a Portuguese announcer declaring, "Senhoras e senhores, medalhistas olímpicos" (Ladies and gentlemen, Olympic medalists). He stands on the podium with silver medalist Kosuke Hagino and bronze medalist Wang Shun, all smiling with their medals. A text overlay highlights Phelps' fourth consecutive 200m IM win, beating the field by at least 1.95 seconds, as an upbeat, heroic instrumental track with a strong beat and synth elements begins to play. The segment then shifts to a title card for the 'Men's 50m Freestyle,' followed by a wide shot of the brightly lit Olympic arena and starting blocks, where swimmers like N. Adrian in lane 6 are preparing for the next race, with the music continuing. A commentator briefly interjects over the music, stating, "He can't help but be affected by..." before the audio cuts off.

As they near the finish, on-screen graphics display 'WR 49.82' and 'OR 50.58'. The commentators erupt with excitement, declaring, 'History because Singapore has won an Olympic gold medal and an Olympic record time!' The race concludes with Joseph Schooling setting a new Olympic Record of 50.39, which the commentator calls 'simply sensational.' He is shown in a close-up celebrating in the water, removing his goggles and smiling triumphantly, while the crowd erupts in audible cheers, with fans waving Singaporean flags. The video then transitions to the podium ceremony, where the commentators confirm a unique 'triple way second' finish. Joseph Schooling, described as a '21-year-old Singaporean... what a superstar!' who trains at the University of Texas in Austin, stands in the center wearing a green and yellow Singaporean tracksuit with his gold medal. He is flanked by silver medalists Chad Guy Bertrand LE CLOS of South Africa and Michael Phelps of the USA, both named by the commentator, along with Lazlo Cseh, who also received a silver. Phelps is in a navy USA jacket with red sleeves, and Le Clos in a green and yellow tracksuit. The trio raises their hands in unison under the bright stadium lighting and Rio 2016 branding, celebrating this grand Olympic moment.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with an excited announcer describing Michael Phelps and his team's "extraordinary record of never having lost the medley" as Phelps, shirtless and beaming, celebrates their Rio 2016 Men's 4 x 100m Medley Relay victory with teammates like Ryan Murphy, all in USA swimwear, laughing, embracing, and waving to the cheering crowd. The scene transitions to the U.S. team, now in matching navy and red USA jackets, proudly holding their gold medals on the podium, flanked by silver medalists from Great Britain and bronze from Australia, with an on-screen graphic confirming the results. The announcer hails Phelps as "the greatest Olympian." A female voice, possibly a team member, then states, "It means a lot being part of this relay team as the last time it happens," before the announcer adds, "Well, we're not sure what might happen here." The video then cuts to a blue wooden-textured screen, accompanied by an upbeat pop song with male vocals singing "You're a superhero." White text on screen details 'Phelps' career ended with an Olympic record (If he doesn't come back)' and lists 'Crazy splits' for Ryan Murphy (51.85 WR), Adam Peaty (56.59), Kyle Chalmers (46.72), and Nathan Adrian (46.74). The segment concludes with a celebratory animation of a swimmer emerging from water amidst confetti, followed by hand-drawn 'THANKS FOR WATCHING' and a 'PLEASE LIKE & SUBSCRIBE' call-to-action against a snowy backdrop, all set to the energetic pop music.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens with a jubilant scene of Michael Phelps and his teammates celebrating their victory in the Men's 4 x 100m Medley Relay at the Rio 2016 Olympics. Phelps, shirtless and beaming, is seen laughing and embracing his teammates, including Ryan Murphy, who is also shirtless and smiling broadly. The team, clad in blue and red USA swimwear, walks along the pool deck, waving to the cheering crowd in the packed arena. The camera follows Phelps as he raises his arms triumphantly, acknowledging the fans. The scene transitions to the victory ceremony, where the U.S. team stands on the podium in matching navy and red USA jackets, proudly holding their gold medals. They are flanked by the silver medalists from Great Britain and bronze medalists from Australia, with all teams shaking hands in sportsmanship. The on-screen graphic confirms the results: United States of America (Gold), Great Britain (Silver), Australia (Bronze). The video then cuts to a blue wooden-textured screen with white text stating, 'Phelps' career ended with an Olympic record (If he doesn't come back),' followed by a list of 'Crazy splits' detailing each swimmer's leg: Ryan Murphy's 51.85 (WR), Adam Peaty's 56.59, Kyle Chalmers' 46.72, and Nathan Adrian's 46.74. The segment concludes with a celebratory animation of a swimmer emerging from the water amidst confetti, with the text 'CONGRATS TO THE SWIMMERS!!' followed by a hand-drawn 'THANKS FOR WATCHING' and a final call-to-action screen with 'PLEASE LIKE & SUBSCRIBE' in bold red and black text against a snowy backdrop.
